paper_title: Cognitive Episodes in LLM Reasoning Traces Enable Interpretable Human Item Difficulty Prediction

research_equation.problem: Predicting human item difficulty from assessment items with interpretable process evidence
research_equation.input: Assessment item text + LRM reasoning traces (from QwQ-32B, Qwen3-32B under 4 solver profiles)
research_equation.transformation: Sentence-level episode profiling → Episode-induced process features (length, episode distribution, transition) + Sentence-BERT semantic embedding → XGBoost
research_equation.output: Difficulty label (Easy/Medium/Hard or continuous IRT b-parameter / p-value)
research_equation.objective: Minimize prediction error while providing interpretable evidence about the cognitive processes underlying difficulty

C1: Costly human calibration.challenge: CTT/IRT requires extensive field testing with hundreds of examinees per item
C1: Costly human calibration.module: Epi2Diff pipeline as automated alternative using LRM-generated traces

C2: Text-only models miss process-level.challenge: Existing text-based models predict difficulty from item text alone, ignoring how an item is solved
C2: Text-only models miss process-level.module: Episode-induced process representation (83-dim) captures reasoning scale, effort allocation, and reasoning flow

C3: Raw reasoning traces are noisy and u.challenge: LRM traces are long, redundant, and noisy; directly using them degrades performance
C3: Raw reasoning traces are noisy and u.module: Sentence-level episode classification (RoBERTa, 0.799 ACC) + compact feature extraction (length, episode distribution, transitions)

C4: LLM direct difficulty judgments are.challenge: LLMs show poor alignment with human difficulty; their difficulty estimates reflect model-centric rather than human-centric difficulty (Li et al., 2025a)
C4: LLM direct difficulty judgments are.module: Process-based approach: difficulty is inferred from reasoning trace dynamics rather than direct LLM rating

C5: Single LRM trace may be unrepresenta.challenge: A single reasoning trace may not capture the full difficulty spectrum due to stochasticity
C5: Single LRM trace may be unrepresenta.module: Multi-profile aggregation: average process features across weak/medium/strong simulated students + 2 LRM sources (8 traces per item)

C6: Episode labeling requires costly hum.challenge: Manual episode annotation at sentence level is labor-intensive
C6: Episode labeling requires costly hum.module: RoBERTa automatic episode labeler trained on 231,913 GPT-5 annotated sentence–episode pairs with human-verified gold test set

C7: Trade-off between feature richness a.challenge: Richer/higher-dimensional representations do not always improve difficulty prediction (Fulari & Rusert, 2024)
C7: Trade-off between feature richness a.module: Compact 83-dim feature set (3 length + 16 episode distribution + 64 transition) with XGBoost, validated via systematic feature ablation

design_principles: Cognitive grounding: use Schoenfeld's Episode Theory as validated ontology for problem-solving stages
Process ≠ verbosity: explicitly model effort allocation and flow, not just length
Multiple perspectives: aggregate across solver profiles (weak/medium/strong) and LRM sources for robustness
Compactness: 83 feature dimensions avoid noise from high-dimensional raw traces
Complementarity: semantic embeddings + process features capture both 'what' and 'how' of item solving
Interpretability: each feature group (scale/allocation/flow) maps to an interpretable aspect of problem solving
